occurrenceremarks: "Common Goldfields (Lasthenia gracilis) Endemic in California. Native, annual plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family that grows in many plant communities throughout California. It can cover entire grassland fields or hillsides with a brilliant gold carpet that can be seen from a great distance. Stem is simple or freely branched and +- hairy. Leaves are linear to oblanceolate, entire, +- hairy, (+- fleshy in coastal forms). Involucre is bell-shaped or hemispheric, and hairy. There are 6–13 ray flowers and 4–13 free, hairy phyllaries, and many disk flowers.The pappus is lance-ovate in shape and opaque white in color. Peak bloom time: February-May. Flora of Fort Ord: Monterey County, California, David Styer, 2019, p. 49. (D. Styer calls Lasthenia gracilis ""Coast Goldfields"") ""This species is a chaparral edge or woodland edge plant, unlike the other two vernal pool goldfield plants on Fort Ord"") Jepson eFlora (with botanical illustration): https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=79082 Calflora: Wild Plants of California (includes species distribution map) https://www.calflora.org/app/taxon?crn=9143 Flora of North America http://beta.floranorthamerica.org/Lasthenia_gracilis Plants of Monterey County: an Illustrated Field Key, 2nd edition, Matthews and Mitchell, 2015, pp. 70-71. Monterey County Wildflowers: a Field Guide, Yeager and Mitchell, 2016, p. 302. Monterey County Wildflowers– a photographic guide https://montereywildflowers.com/asteraceae-sunflower1/ Stem is simple or freely branched, branching is occasionally basal in desert forms, +- hairy. ""L. gracilis is the correct name for the taxon occuring in southern California and Baja California."" Jon P. Rebman. Baja California Plant Field Guide, Jon P. Rebman, Norman C. Roberts, 3rd. ed, 2012, p. 145. ""This species used to be merged with L. californica, and is the only one in the Aguanga area."" Tom Chester. Anza-Borrego Desert Wildflowers (and more) https://borregowildflowers.org/?type=search&searchtype=C&family=&name=Common%20goldfields Leaf Shape and Arrangement diagrams: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e8/Leaf_morphology.svg"
verbatimlocality: Toro County Park, Salinas, CA, US

identificationremarks: "Pappi match the illustration in Chan (2001) very closely. i.e. pappi are ""ovate-lanceolate scales, each tapering to an awn"". They are not simply ""linear"", as in L. californica. Chan's Figure 1 caption says: ""Different pappus types present in L. californica subsp. californica and L. gracilis. A—clear to brown linear awns (L. californica subsp. californica only); B—opaque, white, ovate-lanceolate scales, each tapering to an awn (L. gracilis only); C—clear to brown subulate awns (L. californica subsp. californica only); D—epappose (both L. californica subsp. californica and L. gracilis)."" @hkibak"
Same population as this one with pappus photographed: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/202927432

occurrenceremarks: "This fuzzy photo is a ""longshot"" taken from a fast moving car. But I've always wondered what these pretty yellow fields of flowers are that blanket the hills near Aguanga in April. Common Goldfields (Lasthenia gracilis) Endemic in California. Native, annual plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family that grows in many plant communities throughout California. It can cover entire grassland fields or hillsides with a brilliant gold carpet that can be seen from a great distance. Stem is simple or freely branched and +- hairy. Leaves are linear to oblanceolate, entire, +- hairy, (+- fleshy in coastal forms). Involucre is bell-shaped or hemispheric, and hairy. There are 6–13 ray flowers and 4–13 free, hairy phyllaries, and many disk flowers.The pappus is lance-ovate in shape and opaque white in color. Peak bloom time: February-May. Jepson eFlora https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=79082 Calflora: Wild Plants of California (includes species distribution map) https://www.calflora.org/app/taxon?crn=9143 Flora of North America http://beta.floranorthamerica.org/Lasthenia_gracilis Plants of Monterey County: an Illustrated Field Key, 2nd edition, Matthews and Mitchell, 2015, pp. 70-71. Monterey County Wildflowers: a Field Guide, Yeager and Mitchell, 2016, p. 302. Monterey County Wildflowers– a photographic guide https://montereywildflowers.com/asteraceae-sunflower1/ Flora of Fort Ord: Monterey County, California, David Styer, 2019, p. 49. Stem is simple or freely branched, branching is occasionally basal in desert forms, +- hairy. ""L. gracilis is the correct name for the taxon occuring in southern California and Baja California."" Jon P. Rebman. Baja California Plant Field Guide, Jon P. Rebman, Norman C. Roberts, 3rd. ed, 2012, p. 145. ""This species used to be merged with L. californica, and is the only one in the Aguanga area."" Tom Chester. Anza-Borrego Desert Wildflowers (and more) https://borregowildflowers.org/?type=search&searchtype=C&family=&name=Common%20goldfields Leaf Shape and Arrangement diagrams: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e8/Leaf_morphology.svg"
identificationremarks: This species used to be merged with L. californica, and is the only one in the Aguanga area.
